title: Interview Prep
description: 30+ interview questions at easy, medium, and hard levels — covering concepts, gotchas, and optimization scenarios.

sections.content: These are the topics that come up in virtually every MongoDB interview. Understand the *why*, not just the *what*.

sections.heading: Top 10 must-know concepts

sections.content: **Common interview trap:** 'Is MongoDB ACID compliant?' Answer: Yes — *single-document* operations are always ACID. Multi-document transactions require explicit session/transaction blocks (MongoDB 4.0+). Most PostgreSQL developers assume all databases are always fully ACID — MongoDB is, but with that nuance.

sections.heading: Common performance mistakes

lessons.title: Replica Sets, Sharding, and Write/Read Concerns

sections.content: **Replica sets in depth.** A replica set is 3+ MongoDB instances keeping identical copies of your data. The **primary** receives all writes. **Secondaries** replicate by tailing the **oplog** (operations log) — a capped collection on the primary recording every write. If the primary goes down, secondaries elect a new one automatically using a Raft-based consensus algorithm.

**The oplog** is a special capped collection in the `local` database. Every write to the primary is recorded here as an operation (insert, update, delete). Secondaries continuously read the oplog and apply the same operations to stay in sync. Change Streams (see next lesson) also work by tailing the oplog.

**Write Concern** — how durable must a write be before MongoDB responds? The two most important values are: `w: 1` (default) — the primary has written it. `w: 'majority'` — more than half the replica set has written it. Higher write concern = more durable, slightly higher latency.

sections.heading: Write concern and read concern

sections.content: **Read Preference** controls which replica set member handles reads. `primary` (default) — always read from primary, consistent. `secondaryPreferred` — read from secondaries when available (reduces primary load, but secondaries may be slightly behind = eventual consistency).

**Sharding** is horizontal scaling — splitting your data across multiple servers (shards) based on a shard key. Each shard holds a subset of documents. A `mongos` router sits in front and directs queries to the right shard. Choosing the right shard key is critical: a poorly chosen key creates 'hot spots' where one shard gets all the traffic.

sections.heading: Scaling: PostgreSQL vs MongoDB
table: Add more CPU/RAM to one server

lessons.title: Change Streams, GridFS, and Working Set

sections.content: **Change Streams** let your application listen to real-time database changes — inserts, updates, and deletes — as they happen. Internally they tail the oplog. Use cases: real-time dashboards, push notifications, event-driven architectures, cache invalidation.

sections.heading: Change Streams — listen to real-time changes

sections.content: **GridFS** is MongoDB's mechanism for storing files larger than the 16 MB document limit. It splits the file into 255 KB chunks, stores them in a `fs.chunks` collection, and keeps metadata in `fs.files`. Use GridFS for large videos, audio files, or any binary over 16 MB. For small files, store them as BSON Binary directly in a document.

**The Working Set** is the portion of your data and indexes that is actively read. MongoDB relies heavily on the operating system's memory cache (RAM) for fast reads. If your working set fits in RAM, reads are served from memory and are extremely fast. If it doesn't, MongoDB hits disk on every cache miss — orders of magnitude slower. This is the most important factor in MongoDB production performance and why RAM sizing matters.

**Write Amplification** is a hidden cost of large embedded arrays. When you update a single field inside a large document, MongoDB rewrites the entire document to disk. A 500 KB document with a large embedded array means every small field update writes 500 KB. Moving frequently-updated data to a separate small collection fixes this.

**WiredTiger** has been MongoDB's default storage engine since version 3.2. Key features: document-level locking (many concurrent writes without blocking), Snappy/zlib compression (typically 60–80% size reduction), and multi-version concurrency control (MVCC). The old `mmap` engine was removed in MongoDB 4.2.

**The query planner caches its decisions.** After a query runs, MongoDB caches which index won the 'race'. The cache is invalidated when the collection changes significantly (e.g. many writes) or when you add/drop an index. You can force a specific index with `.hint({ fieldName: 1 })`.

**Index cardinality** is the number of distinct values a field can have. High cardinality (email: millions of values) = selective index = fast queries. Low cardinality (boolean: 2 values) = non-selective = the index may be ignored by the query planner in favour of a collection scan. This is why indexing a boolean field rarely helps.
